original_abstract: Modern scientific experiments increasingly rely on the simultaneous control and monitoring of multiple, heterogeneous data streams. However, because standard computer programming traditionally relies on the serial execution of instructions, developing software that can handle the asynchronous and parallel nature of scientific data remains a significant challenge. To address this, we present Bonsai: a modular, high-performance, open-source visual programming framework designed for the real-time acquisition and processing of diverse data streams. We describe Bonsai’s event-based architecture and core principles, demonstrating how it facilitates the rapid and flexible prototyping of complex experimental designs. We highlight its utility in neuroscience through several practical applications, including high-speed behavioral video tracking, multi-channel electrophysiology, and reactive closed-loop systems for environmental control and stimulation.
humanized_abstract: Today’s scientific experiments routinely involve the simultaneous monitoring and control of multiple data streams originating from a variety of sources. However, because most programming paradigms are based on the sequential execution of instructions on a single processing core, rapidly implementing data flow-oriented scientific software that is capable of dealing with the truly asynchronous and parallel nature of present day scientific data, is still a common obstacle. Bonsai is a modular, high-performance and open-source visual programming framework that can be used for the on-the-fly acquisition and processing of large data streams from multiple data sources. The underlying event-driven architecture and design patterns of Bonsai allow for a very rapid exploration of new experimental setups, which we illustrate with examples from neuroscience such as high frames rate video tracking, multi-channel recording experiments and reactive control of environmental condition and stimulation systems.

Bonsai is a modular, high-performance and open-source visual programming framework that can be used for the on-the-fly acquisition and processing of large data streams from multiple data sources. The underlying event-driven architecture and design patterns of Bonsai allow for a very rapid exploration of new experimental setups, which we illustrate with examples from neuroscience such as high frames rate video tracking, multi-channel recording experiments and reactive control of environmental condition and stimulation systems.
